speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Leopold Zylberman. This is tape five. Leopold, you were talking about this Dutch person in Kovno. Are you-- is this a story you heard or is it something that you experienced yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I heard. But the gossip, sort of went very popular. And everybody started to travel to Kovno. And for 11 litas, which was the Lithuanian currency, first of all, they were traveling through the British embassy, which issued Polish traveling passes. With this, we went to the Dutch embassy. For 11 dollars-- or litas, you know-- which was 2 dollars or so, we got the visa to Curacao. And with this visa to Curacao, we went to the Japanese Consul, and he gave us a visa for 10 days through Japan. The problem was go through Russia. Now, you see, everybody knew. It was a very, very public set, and queues were to pick up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Queues to what?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the consulates, the Polish embassy, and you see, it means I didn't go to Kovno, but you see, a friend of ours fixed me up, you see, with the visas. Eventually everybody had a visa.

speaker: interviewer
text: So are you saying that people could go to Kovno and get a visa for somebody else who wasn't there at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, you see that, especially the Japanese visa. And when-- when the-- in the meantime Russia occupied Lithuania. And they made it the 15th or whatever republic. And all our papers, which were launched in the Soviet Embassy to get the Soviet visa, went to the KGB. At that time it was called NKVD, you know. And we were prepared for the worst, you see. We bought the sheepskins, and sheep, you see, boots, et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because at that time they started to send people from the East Poland down to Siberia. And we were ready to be picked up, and prepared as much as we could with the combined the fork and knife, and all camping gear. And this was going on. And we used to go to the fellow called Mr. Schlossberg for interview. And he kept on talking, listen, it's such a good country and everybody's happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's Mr. Schlossberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the Chief of NKVD, the interviewer of the KGB, which was called NKVD. And you see, we're going home with nothing. And then all of a sudden I was sitting in a cafe, playing checkers. And they said at [NON-ENGLISH], they're giving visas. Because there was a notice on the embassy that the people who applied for visas pulled the passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we arranged to pull the passports. That was Christmas time. What passport did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one issued by the English embassy, traveling passes issued by the British embassy in Kovno, before they were sort of evacuated. And everyone was during the next-- that means that was on Friday. On Monday or so, they brought in-- they started giving out the visas. And that means that was not a visa. That was an interior traveling permit, you see, between Vilna and Vladivostok. And we were going, [INAUDIBLE] permit. And some people who had good connections before, they already went through them. Anyway, I sent my brother to Kovno. And he came back with a very-- message that you can get tickets to Vladivostok. But they ask for American dollars.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were frightened. Let me see, you got the interest with America dollars, and they stop you as a-- as a black marketeer. But somehow, a few people took a chance, and they go on. And we started selling the watches and jewelry, and whatever we could, to get it together. We still didn't have enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you see, the-- the railway, the trains going to Siberia, you see, they were equipped with dining rooms. And so if you pay for a ticket, you paid for the ticket, you paid for dinner and food, and you paid for accommodation in Moscow. And it came to sort of $500-600 a couple. And one Sunday night, they said that we can do it for half the money. Because the train is going away empty, and we'll buy the food, you see, at local prices. And for 10 days we could go buy 2 dozen of tins, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I ran down, I got up at 6 o'clock in the morning, Took a train to Kaunas, and I was in constant connections by phone with Lola, and I queued up there. I was there through the night in the queue. And I had $320. American dollars?

speaker: interviewee
text: American, yeah. I was standing in the queue, and I got the tickets. And I rang Vilna. You see, the night before we bought a suitcase. And we sold the sheepskins and everything. And two friends were helping Lola. Because she was a bit of a slim-muscled girl, who couldn't do it herself. And when they got my form that the tickets are ready, I bought-- they bought platform tickets, and they waited. And then the train was going from Kovno to [NON-ENGLISH], and those two fellows, you see, took the big suitcase and pushed her into the train. And there was six people sleeping, you see. That makes three stories. We had two couples with us. And it was-- there a good time. Because, you see, that means where there was food we could get food for rationed rubles. Apparently you see speaking well, I had to tell my story. But later on, some people went and paid the rubles too. But at first, we were not the first suckers, but the second. And, you see, children were walking you see, could pass from one car to the other, and-- and going to the dining room. And played checkers or cards. And it was sort of a happy time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did the journey last?

speaker: interviewee
text: 11 days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, let me see, in the meantime, you see, Lola was sick. And the train manager wanted to take her off on some station in Siberia. He said, we have a beautiful hospital. So, they say, you better not. Eventually everything was all right. We found some aspirins and things. And then when we landed in Vladivostok, you see, that means the train came to a side track. And immediately from the train, we were taken to a cattle ship, to go from Vladivostok to Tsuruga.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well-- To where, sorry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Go from Vladivostok to Tsuruga to sail.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Tsuruga?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Japan, you see, West coast of Japan. And you see this boat was already organized by Jewish organizations. Because where people were flooded with visas, you see, the [NON-ENGLISH], and the [NON-ENGLISH], and the Bund, and the Zionist; you see. And there was a sort of division of groups. And each one helped each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were put on the cattle ship. You see, there hardly was a sitting place. And you see, we look on the boat, and we are surrounded by ice. You see everything was around white. And I'd say, oh goodness sake. What's going to happen? And before we knew, an icebreaker came. That's a small, but a bit powerful ship. And into the ice, you see, [NON-ENGLISH] and started swimming.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came back. We came-- we were a bit relaxed. And sort of towing little vessel came to take the ship out to the-- to the neutral waters. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and then we came out in the open sea. And people were relaxed. And a fellow just stand his back to the-- back-- his back to shore, and say, now you can kiss me wherever you want, et cetera. And we set in the-- on the ship, I think, for a day and a half or two. And we were fed with curry rice And we never eat curry before. We thought we would burn inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Curry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Curry and rice. Curry and rice, but somehow we survived it. And the-- Japan was beautiful. That means when we saw Japan there was every inch, you see, utilized. You see, that means the whole thing-- you see it was a mountain. It was all in squares, with different colors of green, you know. And then we saw the [NON-ENGLISH] port, walking on the scaffs or whatever you called it then. And a lot of them where masks on the thing. Because I don't know whether it was an epidemic, or it was the usual thing not to get infected. Then we were met by the Jewish organization, and taken to a station, and waited for a train. And we were very hungry. And we saw a beautiful pears, a couple pears and we bought two pears. And we couldn't bite them in. He said, you picked up the wrong fruit. We're hungry again. But when we go to the train and came to Kobe, we were met by the people who came over there 10 days earlier. It was my friends and--

speaker: interviewer
text: People you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They came. They were the same group, but they risked, and they paid the big dollars. They had the big dollars, so they came on the first. Because I went on the third, us alone. And they then gave us instructions. And they said, well, have you got that money or something? You see I said, look, I $50 American hidden in a fountain pen. Everybody say, all right. Here, you've got so many yens, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you exchange your $50 American for yen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not yet. Not yet, because it was-- it was nightfall. But you see that people would trust each other, or something. And they gave me money so I could buy something, go shopping. And they put us to a the Jewish-- it was I think 26 families, Jewish families, in Kobe. You see all importers-exporters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you saying this is an established community in Kobe?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a established community with a Jewish club, you see, [NON-ENGLISH], and all sorts of things. And the people who came earlier got themselves jobs, you see, processing the people who came later. These 26 families were revisionists. So they gave priority to their own political friends. And they put us in-- they bought houses, and they put one mattress next to the other. And every mattress was a couple. We were happy that we were out of Russia, or out of things. So yeah, I guess-- and then the next ship brought my brother and his-- my sister-in-law, and other few friends. And I went-- I just got myself hold of a few dollars. I don't know why. I don't remember what I sold. And I went to a little hotel. No. They were-- they kept the refugees there, you know. But there were Japanese people. And there was a sort of a transient hotel. Well, you slept on the floor, you see, with straw mattresses and something. There was nothing in it. And they gave you a-- a hibachi you see, sort of like a flower pot. You see, on which you cooked with charcoal or briquettes. And there was a happy company that means we had one neighbor on one side, one neighbor on the other. Now there were two boys, two men, you see occupying one room. One was a son of a banker, who was an epileptic. And he had a fellow who looked after him. And this epileptic fellow, he spoke English. And there was a Japanese neighbor. And he was translating the newspaper to English. And we met together at [INAUDIBLE] place, and here that's the news. And we knew everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time, we found out that Hitler started the-- the war with the Soviet Union, that means in June, you see, Barbarossa, whatever you call it. We knew, and we had all the news. We still had correspondence from Japan to the ghetto and back.

speaker: interviewer
text: To which ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Warsaw ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was in-- in June, June which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That means 1941, the beginning of 1941. And we used to send them by post, square parcels of tea. You see, tea was packed the English way, those little cubes, cubic parcels. And they were-- they were receiving the parcels. But the letters were very bad, very, very tragic. But it was hard. But they never expected what was coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you writing to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To my mother. My wife was writing to her sisters. You see, she had two sisters, and a-- and a brother and a couple of parents. We even, at that time, got a photo, a family photo from the ghetto where my father-in-law wore one of my suits that I left behind. It was a navy blue suit with green stripes, navy and green for the first time in '39, they started matching green and navy, you know. And Chaim was wearing this suit. Well, we had to look after our own skin. And in the morning, we go get the train, the tram actually. And going to the committee, and just waiting for news. They had, of course, then we were going to [NON-ENGLISH], and riding the escalator. You know, that means this-- it was a new world open to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feed-- feed yourselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: That means the Jewish committee used to give us a yen 20 a day, you know. And we had a few bob, you see, ate up the saving. And eventually we came out without jewelry, without watches, et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you spend in Kobe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you spend in Kobe?

speaker: interviewee
text: That means we spent in Kobe till end of August.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you do any work while you were there at all, any sort of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No, there was no-- no work whatsoever. Because you couldn't compete with the Japs, you see. But we were-- you see that means the fares on the trains were next to nothing. We're driving here and there, and you see there was the war time. There was no rice. So we get-- got some sort of a porridge.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the local people treat you? Were they aware that-- of-- of your Jewish identity, what it was to be Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: They wouldn't know. They wouldn't know. But you see, that means we went to the market. And that was very funny, you know, that means now and again, is they stop all the business, and the music was playing, and the Japanese were doing exercises, you know. And when you see-- they saw sort of a couple with a baby, they called the baby in, and they gave it a couple of eggs. Because you see that was black market. And we sort of by mimic, we knew a few words, you see. That means we knew-- I don't know. I got mixed up with the Japanese with-- with the Brazilian. Everything is sort of can't, you see, recall any words. You see, we learned 1, 2, 3, 4. We paid the tickets, and we go-- went down to the [NON-ENGLISH], look at the-- at the geishas.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, how would you describe your period in Kobe generally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beautiful. Yeah, and at that time, the-- the Polish embassy was acting in Kobe, in Tokyo. And we used to go to the festival event to the Polish consulate in Osaka to register ourselves that we escaped to Japan, and we want to volunteer to the Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish exile Army, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were going to volunteer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just to get out. You know, and a lot of people volunteered. But they never got out to the army. Because you see, as I said, we come to the-- some to this. You see, they needed good soldiers. We were not-- you see, I had no commission. I had no training. And you see the Japanese ambassador, the secretary, and he was interviewing everybody, and this and that. And I said, I am Zylberman. And he knew Henry. But nothing came out of it. You see, they were getting visas. You see, there was-- there were two problems. First of all, was the visas. Second, if you had a visa it had to have the transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was just like they bought out all the tickets, all the visas. And they started sending out. First they sent the Poles out. Because it was in this group there was quite a few Poles. Where were they sending them?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Canada, to Australia, South Africa, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had-- you had a visa from the Dutch in Kovno to take you to--

speaker: interviewee
text: But that visa wasn't valid. Because Curacao, you had to have a permit from the government. We knew that clearly. And-- and that wasn't a place to live. It was sort of tropic and Caribbean.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you plan to go with Lola?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I wanted to go to either to Canada or to Australia, to America was out of the question. Because the Polish [NON-ENGLISH] was [NON-ENGLISH] behind. So they send out. They started, you see, when they had shipment transport. They first send out the officers. You know, because they were as I say, officers. They will send out the doctors. They send out the engineering, you see. And they send out the people who could bribe the secretary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if a few rich Jews, you see, came to Australia. And there was some 35 visas available for Burma. For?

speaker: interviewee
text: Burma, Burma Rangoon. And we applied to go to Rangoon out of there. And he said that he can't send out the whole family. He won't-- cannot give us two visas. So my brother and his wife went, you see. There-- because my brother explained to me that he's smarter. And as soon as he comes, he will arrange. Because he was the oldest brother. He was bossing me around. And I sort of got used to be pushed around. And he went to Burma. And priorities, priorities, priorities, you see let me see if this business could last another year, the next 35 people, I would go. But the Japanese lost patience, and they said we're finished with it. And they arranged transport, you see ship to Shanghai. And Shanghai was at that time a neutral zone. There was a French Concession. And there was the international settlement. And it was a Japanese Concession.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your thoughts on going to Shanghai?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no thoughts. I had to go and that's it. And already some people were sent out before. So again, we were met, you see by friends who gave us sort of a guidance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in Shanghai.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you to Shanghai?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, when we landed in the Huangpu River.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, by a very nice freighter. And we came back, and we were met by a few friends, who gave us the thing. And I don't know. We spent a couple of nights, you see, somewhere in transit. And then we went out to find accommodation. Shanghai was very cheap. And there was a lot of German Jews, you see, who came to Shanghai as migrants. You see, and they came overloaded. Because they were allowed to take out everything. And some was the professional, the rich people, who established themselves in the French Concession or the international settlement, bought shop, et cetera. But the majority of them was housed in old warehouses or factories, on three-story high beds.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're just going to take a break there, and change tapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
